summary: It's been a crazy Monday with ICE agents showing up at Hartsfield-Jackson to help with the TSA chaos from the government shutdown. Over in Reynoldstown, that long-empty corner near the BeltLine is finally getting somewhere with new construction, and Inman Park just welcomed a fresh Asian-American comfort food spot.

topStory.headline: Reynoldstown Corner Near BeltLine Finally Breaks Ground After 5 Years
topStory.summary: That empty lot at Chester Avenue and Fulton Terrace where buildings got torn down back in 2021 is finally showing signs of life. Embry Development is moving forward with 85 residential units about a block from the Eastside Trail. It's been a long wait for neighbors who've watched that corner sit vacant.
topStory.body:
You know that cleared corner at Chester Avenue and Fulton Terrace in Reynoldstown? The one that's been sitting empty since developers knocked down whatever was there almost five years ago? Well, finally, something's happening.

Embry Development Company has started construction on what they're calling mixed townhomes and for-sale condos. They bought the 2-acre site from Toll Brothers two years ago and are moving forward with the permitted 85 residential units. It's about a block north of Memorial Drive and maybe a five-minute walk to the Eastside Trail, so the location is pretty solid for anyone wanting to be close to the BeltLine action.

This kind of thing has been happening all over the SE BeltLine corridor. Developers buy sites, sometimes sit on them for years while the market does whatever it's doing, then finally build when the timing feels right. For neighbors who've been staring at that empty lot, it's probably a relief to see some movement, even if it means more construction noise for a while.
topStory.imageAlt: Construction site in Reynoldstown showing early development work

stories.headline: Decatur Gets 18 New 'Missing Middle' Houses Near Commercial Strip
stories.summary: Cottages at Midway is about to soft-launch on Midway Road in unincorporated Decatur. It's designed as more accessible ITP housing where median home values have hit over $700,000. The village-style layout around shared courtyards is a nice touch for the area.
stories.body:
With Decatur home prices pushing past $700,000, any project promising 'attainable' housing gets attention. Cottages at Midway is doing just that with 18 standalone houses built around shared courtyards and greenspace on Midway Road.

The mixed-income community is replacing two underused lots on 2.1 acres. The developers are calling it 'missing middle' housing, which basically means it's denser than typical single-family but not as intense as apartment complexes. The village-style setup with shared courtyards feels like a throwback to older neighborhood designs.

With a soft launch starting next week, it'll be interesting to see how 'attainable' actually translates to pricing. Decatur has gotten expensive fast, and anything that helps people stay in the area without breaking the bank is worth paying attention to. The location near a major commercial corridor doesn't hurt either.
stories.imageAlt: Site plan showing village-style housing layout with courtyards

stories.headline: New Asian-American Comfort Food Spot Opens in Inman Park
stories.summary: Mushi Ni just opened today on Elizabeth Street, right off the Freedom Park Trail. Chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez are bringing soft shell crab bao, Peking chicken platters, and Thai tea martinis to the neighborhood. It's their first restaurant after doing the pop-up circuit.
stories.body:
Inman Park gets another food option today with Mushi Ni opening on Elizabeth Street. The location is prime, just off the Freedom Park Trail where people are always walking and biking through. Chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez have been working toward this moment for a while, doing pop-ups around town.

The menu sounds solid for the neighborhood. They've got various bao like soft shell crab and chicken longanisa, plus snacks like umami fries and crab Rangoon puffs. The Peking chicken platter feeds two for $25, which feels reasonable for Inman Park these days. Thai tea martinis and other cocktails round out the offerings for both lunch and dinner.

Both chefs bring serious experience. Jimenez worked for Gordon Ramsay in New York before moving to Atlanta, so they know what they're doing. It'll be interesting to see how the neighborhood receives another Asian-American concept, especially one that's accessible for both quick bites and sit-down meals.
stories.imageAlt: Peking chicken dish from Mushi Ni restaurant

stories.headline: Lewis Barbecue Brings Austin-Style BBQ to Ansley Mall Near BeltLine
stories.summary: The smokehouse at Ansley Mall has a unique setup with six 22-foot smokers in a loft with panoramic windows overlooking the new pedestrian bridge to the BeltLine. John Lewis, the pitmaster, helped weld the smokers from 1,000-gallon propane tanks. Early word is the brisket is some of the best you'll find in Atlanta.
stories.body:
Lewis Barbecue just opened at Ansley Mall behind The Cook's Warehouse, and the setup is unlike anything else in Atlanta. They put their smokehouse upstairs in a loft with huge windows overlooking the new pedestrian bridge to the BeltLine. Six massive smokers run 24 hours a day in eight-hour shifts, making it look like the fanciest engine room in Georgia.

Pitmaster John Lewis helped weld those smokers out of 1,000-gallon propane tanks, so this isn't some corporate franchise operation. He's bringing his Austin and Charleston experience to Atlanta, and early reports say the brisket is legitimately excellent. The location near the BeltLine connection doesn't hurt either.

The timing feels right for serious barbecue in that area. Ansley Mall has been picking up steam with The Cook's Warehouse and other food-focused businesses, and having quality barbecue walking distance from the BeltLine should draw plenty of traffic. Plus, watching the smoking operation through those windows adds some theater to the whole experience.
stories.imageAlt: Barbecue platter with brisket and sides

stories.headline: ICE Agents Show Up at Hartsfield-Jackson as TSA Lines Spiral
stories.summary: Federal immigration officers are walking around the airport to help with security as the partial government shutdown continues. TSA agents have been working without pay since mid-February, and nearly 41% called out on Sunday in Atlanta. Travelers are reporting four-hour wait times, but the ICE agents can't actually do much.
stories.body:
The scene at Hartsfield-Jackson has gotten pretty wild. Armed federal agents in tactical gear are patrolling the terminals, not for immigration enforcement but supposedly to help with airport security as TSA faces massive staffing shortages. Nearly 41% of TSA agents called out on Sunday alone in Atlanta, and travelers are dealing with four-hour waits to get through security.

But here's the thing: travelers are saying the ICE agents are mostly just standing around. They can't actually screen passengers or do the TSA's job, so it's unclear how much help they're really providing. Mayor Dickens announced their deployment Sunday while assuring residents they won't engage in immigration enforcement, but the whole situation feels like security theater more than practical help.

Meanwhile, Delta has suspended its special services for members of Congress, making lawmakers wait in the same brutal lines as everyone else. The partial shutdown has been going since mid-February, and there's no clear end in sight. If you're flying through Atlanta anytime soon, pack your patience and maybe some snacks.
stories.imageAlt: Long security lines at Atlanta airport with travelers waiting

stories.headline: Decatur's West Howard Avenue Gets Complete Streets Makeover
stories.summary: The nearly decade-long project to turn West Howard Avenue from a high-speed drag into a people-friendly street is almost done. The one-mile stretch between Downtown Decatur and East Lake MARTA now has sidewalks, bike lanes with concrete barriers, and traffic calming measures. It's a solid example of Complete Streets design.
stories.body:
West Howard Avenue has been a drag strip for cars for years, but Decatur finally finished transforming it into something more civilized. The Complete Streets project runs about one mile from Commerce Drive near Decatur High School to the city limits at Paden Circle by East Lake MARTA station.

The big addition is a two-way cycle track with concrete barriers for most of the route, plus new sidewalks, infrastructure upgrades, and traffic calming measures. It took nearly a decade to get this done, but the result looks pretty good. The concrete barriers should actually protect cyclists instead of just painting lines and hoping for the best.

This kind of infrastructure investment matters for connecting neighborhoods to transit and making streets safer for everyone. The timing works well with East Lake MARTA station right there, giving people a real alternative to driving everywhere. Other cities should take notes on how Decatur approached this project.
stories.imageAlt: New bike lanes with concrete barriers on West Howard Avenue

stories.headline: City Council Advances ICE Limits While Firefighters Demand Contract
stories.summary: The Public Safety Committee unanimously moved forward resolutions to bar ICE detention facilities in Atlanta and require documentation when ICE agents are present during police operations. Meanwhile, dozens of people criticized Mayor Dickens for refusing to sign the first-ever firefighter union contract that council already ratified.
stories.body:
Atlanta City Council had a busy Public Safety Committee meeting with two big issues on the table. The ICE-related resolutions passed unanimously and head to the full council vote on April 20. One would ban ICE detention facilities within city limits, and the other requires Atlanta police to document when ICE agents are present during their operations.

But the bigger drama was around the firefighter union contract. Dozens of people showed up to criticize Mayor Dickens for refusing to sign the city's first-ever contract with the Atlanta firefighters' union, even though council ratified it back in April 2025. The timing is awkward given the current airport situation with federal agents deployed around the city.

The ICE resolutions feel like Atlanta trying to assert some local control over federal immigration enforcement, especially given all the uncertainty around Trump's policies. But the firefighter contract dispute shows the mayor and council still have plenty of their own issues to work out, regardless of what's happening at the federal level.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta City Council meeting with public speakers

stories.headline: 8-Foot Python Found Along Atlanta BeltLine Sparks Wildlife Warning
stories.summary: Someone spotted and handled an 8- to 10-foot snake on the BeltLine, but wildlife officials are warning people not to handle animals they find. The snake appears to be a python, which definitely isn't native to Georgia. Nobody knows if it escaped or was abandoned by an owner.
stories.body:
The Atlanta BeltLine has seen some interesting wildlife over the years, but an 8-foot python is probably a first. Video shows someone yanking the snake out of bushes and laying it across the trail, which wildlife experts say is exactly what you shouldn't do.

Southeastern Reptile Rescue's Jason Clark called the video 'very concerning,' pointing out that handling unknown snakes can be dangerous for both people and the animal. Pythons aren't native to Georgia, so this one either escaped from someone's home or got dumped when an owner couldn't handle it anymore.

Environmental officials are using this as a reminder that people should call professionals instead of trying to handle wildlife themselves. The BeltLine gets tons of foot traffic, so having a large snake hanging out in the bushes could have ended badly. If you see something unusual on the trail, maybe just take photos and call someone who knows what they're doing.
stories.imageAlt: Large python being handled on the Atlanta BeltLine

stories.headline: SW Atlanta Residents Block Alcohol Sales at Controversial Gas Station
stories.summary: Neighbors in NPU P have been fighting a planned gas station on Greenbriar Parkway SW, and they just scored a win when the developer withdrew the alcohol license application. The community argues the gas station violates city law banning new ones in their neighborhood, but construction continues anyway.
stories.body:
Faye Floyd, who chairs Neighborhood Planning Unit P, got some unexpected good news in her email just hours before a key zoning meeting. The developer behind that controversial gas station planned for 3397 Greenbriar Parkway SW had withdrawn their application for an alcohol license.

The community has been fighting this gas station since last year, arguing it violates city law that bans new gas stations in their neighborhood. While they haven't stopped the actual construction, preventing alcohol sales feels like a meaningful victory. Floyd called it a breakthrough in their ongoing dispute with the city over enforcement of its own rules.

This kind of neighborhood organizing shows how residents can still have influence over development, even when city officials seem to ignore their own ordinances. SW Atlanta has dealt with plenty of unwanted development over the years, so residents know they have to stay organized and keep the pressure on. The gas station fight isn't over, but at least it won't be selling liquor too.
stories.imageAlt: Community meeting with residents discussing zoning issues
